attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- cma-doctor: Validate Concept Model Kit installation
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-normalize: Apply whitespace normalization to concept model documents
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-validate: Validate concept model links and annotations
- cma-render: Transform concept model to ClaudeMark format
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	## Ann's PHI Clipbuddy Kit (APCK)
2	
3	APCK is a Tauri desktop app that intercepts clipboard content from Epic EHR, detects PHI via a three-tier engine (regex, label-anchored, dictionary), presents a red/yellow/grey triage view, and writes anonymized plain text to clipboard for pasting into Open Evidence.
4	
5	### File Acronym Mappings
6	
7	| Acronym | File | Description |
8	|---------|------|-------------|
9	| **APCAS** | `apck/APCAS-Specification.md` | Application spec — UX, workflow, deployment |
10	| **APCS0** | `apck/APCS0-SpecTop.adoc` | Detection pipeline spec — formal vocabulary (MCM concept model) |
11	| **APCPS** | `apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` | Prototype spec — tech stack, data sources, project structure |
12	| **APCNS0** | `apck/APCNS0-NeuralStanford.md` | Neural Stanford spike doc — model card, export procedure, observations |
13	| **APCW** | `apck/apcw_workbench.sh` | Workbench |
14	| **APCZ** | `apck/apcz_zipper.sh` | Zipper enrollment |
15	| **APCC** | `apck/apcc_cli.sh` | CLI command implementations |
16	| **APCAP** | `apck/apcd/src/apcap_main.rs` | Tauri app entry point |
17	| **APCAL** | `apck/apcd/src/apcal_main.rs` | Fixture loader (clipboard writer) |
18	| **APCAD** | `apck/apcd/src/apcad_main.rs` | Dictionary refresh (downloads public sources, regenerates dictionaries) |
19	| **APCAB** | `apck/apcd/src/apcab_main.rs` | Batch assay (run detection pipeline on HTML directory, write assay output) |
20	| **APCNSA** | `apck/apcd/src/apcnsa_main.rs` | Neural Stanford assay — spike binary running StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base via ONNX Runtime |
21	
22	**Source modules:**
23	
24	| Source | Prefix | Test | Prefix | Purpose |
25	|--------|--------|------|--------|---------|
26	| `apcrl_log.rs` | `apcrl` | — | — | Logging macros (`apcrl_info!`, `apcrl_error!`, `apcrl_fatal!`) + optional file-tee sink via `apcrl_tee_init` |
27	| `apcre_engine.rs` | `apcre` | `apcte_engine.rs` | `apcte` | PHI detection orchestrator |
28	| `apcrp_parse.rs` | `apcrp` | `apctp_parse.rs` | `apctp` | HTML clipboard parsing |
29	| `apcrm_match.rs` | `apcrm` | `apctm_match.rs` | `apctm` | Dictionary/regex matching |
30	| `apcrd_dictionaries.rs` | `apcrd` | `apctd_dictionaries.rs` | `apctd` | Dictionary loading |
31	| `apcru_update.rs` | `apcru` | — | — | Self-update watcher (no unit tests — I/O + process) |
32	| `apcrh_harvest.rs` | `apcrh` | `apcth_harvest.rs` | `apcth` | Clipboard harvest orchestrator — creates journal dir, scans next index, delegates flavor enumeration to `apcrb_pasteboard` |
33	| `apcrj_journal.rs` | `apcrj` | — | — | Journal directory path resolver — `$HOME/apcjd/` holds harvests + `apcap.log` |
34	| `apcrb_pasteboard.rs` | `apcrb` | `apctb_pasteboard.rs` | `apctb` | macOS NSPasteboard FFI — enumerates the first item's declared UTIs via `objc2-app-kit`, writes each `dataForType` payload to `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}`; non-macOS stub returns an honest error |
35	
36	**Other key paths:**
37	- `Tools/apck/apcd/ui/` — Frontend (HTML/CSS only — no JavaScript)
38	- `Tools/apck/apcd/dictionaries/` — Blacklist/whitelist data files
39	- `Tools/apck/test_fixtures/` — Synthetic Epic clipboard data
40	
41	**No JavaScript rule:** All rendering logic lives in Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface — Rust pushes complete HTML on every state change via `eval()`. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch. No `.js` files, no JS application logic. This eliminates the Rust-JS language boundary as a bug class.
42	
43	**Rust conventions:** Follow RCG (`Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md`). Source prefix: `apcr{classifier}_{name}.rs`. Test prefix: `apct{classifier}_{name}.rs`. Classifier matches between source and test.
44

47	| Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose |
48	|-----------|----------|---------|

50	| `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo run --bin apcap` (local development) |
51	| `tt/apcw-D.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-D` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` |
52	| `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard |
53	| `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` |
54	| `tt/apcw-dr.DictionaryRefresh.sh` | `apcw-dr` | `cargo run --bin apcad` (refresh dictionaries from public sources) |
55	| `tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh` | `apcw-ba` | `cargo run --bin apcab` (batch assay on HTML directory) |
56	| `tt/apcw-nsi.NeuralStanfordInstall.sh` | `apcw-nsi` | Neural Stanford spike — convergent install: create venv, install optimum + optimum-onnx + onnxruntime if needed, clear any prior artifacts, re-export ONNX. Always reaches a working state. |
57	| `tt/apcw-nsa.NeuralStanfordAssay.sh` | `apcw-nsa` | Neural Stanford spike — `cargo run --bin apcnsa` on HTML directory |
58

60	
61	```
62	apc  (non-terminal)
63	├── apca   (non-terminal)
64	│   ├── apcab  — App Batch binary (assay — detection pipeline on HTML files)
65	│   ├── apcad  — App Dictionary binary (refresh from public sources)
66	│   ├── apcal  — App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool)
67	│   ├── apcap  — App Prototype binary (Tauri main)
68	│   └── apcas  — application specification document (UX, workflow)
69	├── apcc   — CLI command implementations
70	├── apcd   — Rust/Tauri source directory
71	│   ├── apcrb  — macOS NSPasteboard FFI — declared-UTI enumeration for harvest
72	│   ├── apcrh  — Clipboard harvest orchestrator (delegates enumeration to apcrb)
73	│   ├── apcrj  — Journal directory path resolver
74	│   └── apcrl  — Logging macros (info, error, fatal with file/line) + file-tee sink
75	├── apcj   (non-terminal — journal)
76	│   └── apcjd  — journal directory ($HOME/apcjd/) holding harvests + apcap.log
77	├── apck   — kit directory
78	├── apcn   (non-terminal — neural)
79	│   └── apcns  (non-terminal — neural stanford)
80	│       ├── apcns0  — Neural Stanford spike documentation (APCNS0-NeuralStanford.md)
81	│       └── apcnsa  — App Neural Stanford Assay binary (ONNX Runtime + stanford-deidentifier-base)
82	├── apcps  — prototype specification document

84	│   └── apcs0  — detection pipeline specification (MCM concept model)
85	├── apcu   (non-terminal)
86	│   └── apcua — update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/)
87	├── apcw   — workbench
88	└── apcz   — zipper
89	```
90	
91	### Detection Architecture
92	
93	Three tiers, merged by highest severity:
94	- **Tier 1 — Regex (RED):** SSN, phone, email, dates, addresses, zip, labeled identifiers
95	- **Tier 2 — Label-anchored (RED):** Words following Epic labels (Patient:, Attending:, Facility:, etc.)
96	- **Tier 3 — Dictionary (YELLOW/PASS):** aho-corasick blacklist scan, whitelist filter, collision → YELLOW
97	
98	### Deploy Workflow
99	
100	1. `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` — produces `.app` bundle
101	2. `tt/apcw-D.Deploy.sh` — scp to Ann's machine at `/Users/Shared/apcua/`
102	3. App self-updates: watches staging directory, copies new bundle over self, relaunches

content.text:
Easiest is a pipe — no intermediate file:

```
docker save myimage:tag | ssh anns-macbook-air 'docker load'
```

If the image is large, compress in flight:

```
docker save myimage:tag | gzip | ssh anns-macbook-air 'gunzip | docker load'
```

Both require Docker's daemon running on each end. Tag resolution carries over, so `docker images` on her machine will show `myimage:tag` ready to run.

content.text:
Docker.app is installed (2.2GB, real bundle). Two things to do on her machine:

1. **Eject the leftover DMG** — the "Docker" volume in `/Volumes/Docker` is still mounted, which is probably why she thinks the install didn't finish. Drag it to trash or run `hdiutil detach /Volumes/Docker`.
2. **Launch it** — open Launchpad or Spotlight ("Docker") and click Docker.app. First launch triggers a Gatekeeper prompt and asks for admin password to install the privileged helper; Spotlight usually clears Gatekeeper's "downloaded from the internet" quarantine cleanly.

Want me to eject the DMG and launch Docker Desktop remotely?

content.text: SSH sessions can't launch GUI apps into Ann's Aqua session directly — the login domain doesn't permit it. We need to push the launch into her GUI session via `launchctl asuser`:

**Short answer: no, not from your current SSH login.** One small change is required.

What's in place already:
- Docker Desktop is running; `/var/run/docker.sock` exists; `/usr/local/bin/docker` is symlinked.

What's blocking you:
- You SSH into Ann's Mac as user `bhyslop`, but the daemon socket is owned by `annsegeren:staff` with mode `srwxr-xr-x` — only `annsegeren` can write to it.
- Docker Desktop is not exposed on any TCP port, so there's no alternate endpoint.

Cheapest fix (one-time, no Docker-side config):
- Get SSH working as **`annsegeren`** (add your public key to `/Users/annsegeren/.ssh/authorized_keys` on her machine, and an alias `annsegeren@anns-macbook-air` in your `~/.ssh/config`). Then:
  ```
  docker save myimage:tag | ssh annsegeren@anns-mba docker load
  ssh annsegeren@anns-mba docker run …
  ```
  Works without touching Docker Desktop settings.

Alternatives if that's not possible: enable Docker Desktop's "Expose daemon on tcp://localhost:2375" (app-side setting — counts as "additional setup"), or run docker via `sudo -u annsegeren` on her Mac (needs her password or a sudoers entry).

content.text:
Technically yes, but it won't stick.

- You're in `admin`, so `sudo chmod 666 /Users/annsegeren/.docker/run/docker.sock` (or `sudo chown bhyslop …`) would let you connect immediately.
- **But Docker Desktop recreates the socket on every VM start** with the owning user's perms, so your change survives only until she reboots / quits Docker / puts the Mac to sleep long enough for the VM to stop.
- You'd also need her admin password each time (`sudo -n` already proved no passwordless sudo), which means a human-in-the-loop on every Docker restart.

So it works as a one-shot unblock, not as a steady state.

For steady state, the three durable options ranked by how "invisible" they are to Ann:

1. **SSH as `annsegeren`** (add your pubkey to her `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`) — zero Docker-side config, no sudo, survives reboots. Cleanest.
2. **Docker Desktop → Settings → Advanced → "Expose daemon on tcp://localhost:2375"** — then `docker -H ssh://bhyslop@anns-mba` or `-H tcp://…` over an SSH tunnel. One-click in the app.
3. **NOPASSWD sudoers entry** for `bhyslop` to run `docker` as `annsegeren` — most invasive.

If you want, I can try the one-shot `sudo chmod` now to confirm it gets you through today's work.

I've found relevant search results about Docker Desktop for Mac socket sharing and multi-user access. Here's what the search results reveal:

## Socket Configuration on Docker Desktop for Mac

Docker Desktop for Mac allows you to enable installation of the /var/run/docker.sock symlink, which ensures various Docker clients relying on the default Docker socket path work without additional changes. Docker Desktop sets up a launchd startup task that creates the symlink by running ln -s -f /Users/<user>/.docker/run/docker.sock /var/run/docker.sock.

## Multi-User Access Challenges

There's a known limitation with multi-user access. When running Docker Desktop for Mac with multiple admin user accounts, the second user account encounters permission denied errors when trying to connect to the Docker daemon socket, even though the first user where Docker Desktop was installed can run docker ps successfully.

Additionally, Docker is not designed to be securely shared among multiple users.

## Remote Access Options

You can configure Docker to accept requests from remote clients by configuring it to listen on an IP address and port as well as the Unix socket. For SSH-based remote access, you can export DOCKER_HOST=ssh://docker-user@host1.example.com to connect to a remote Docker engine.

The search results don't contain specific 2026-related updates, suggesting this is a long-standing architectural limitation in Docker Desktop for Mac that hasn't been fundamentally changed in recent releases.

content.text:
## Findings

**The GUI "Expose daemon on tcp://localhost:2375" toggle has never existed on macOS** — it's a Windows-only Docker Desktop setting. Not a v29.4.0 change; Docker has acknowledged this gap for years.

**Docker Desktop for Mac is explicitly per-user.** From Docker's own docs/issues: the socket belongs to whichever user launched Docker Desktop, and a second account on the same Mac gets permission-denied. Docker states it "is not designed to be securely shared among multiple users" — this is architectural, not a setting.

## What actually works on 29.4.0 (no Docker Desktop config change)

**Option 1 — `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://…` (Docker's own recommended remote transport)**
Docker CLI tunnels over SSH automatically. Once you can SSH as `annsegeren`:
```
export DOCKER_HOST=ssh://annsegeren@anns-macbook-air
docker ps
docker save myimage:tag | docker -H ssh://annsegeren@anns-macbook-air load
```
No TCP port, no socket chmod, no daemon reconfig. This is the clean path.

**Option 2 — socat sidecar (TCP bridge inside a container)**
Run a privileged container on her Mac that mounts the Unix socket and republishes it on a TCP port; then connect from your Mac via an SSH tunnel. This is the community workaround specifically because the macOS GUI lacks the TCP toggle. More moving parts; only worth it if SSH-as-annsegeren is off the table.

**Option 3 — chmod the socket**
Works momentarily, gets wiped on every Docker Desktop / VM restart. Not a steady state.

## Recommendation

Get your SSH key into `/Users/annsegeren/.ssh/authorized_keys` and use `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://…`. One-time setup on her side, zero Docker-side reconfig, survives reboots, no password prompts.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about running Docker Desktop on Mac with multiple user accounts:

## Key Limitation

Docker Desktop can only be run by one user-account per machine, namely the one specified in the --user flag. This is an important architectural limitation of Docker Desktop for Mac.

## The Problem with Multiple Users

When running docker commands from a different user account on the same Macbook, the commands cannot be run, and even running with sudo the commands cannot be run—for example, running sudo docker ps results in "Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock".

After installing Docker, or updating, the docker cli commands are only accessible to the user which initiated the install/update process.

## Workarounds

Some users have documented workarounds to enable multi-user access:

- One workaround is to remove /usr/local/bin/docker and replace it with a symlink directly to /Applications/Docker.app/Contents/Resources/bin/docker

- Creating a docker user that does not need admin access, and ensuring that this user is automatically logged into in Settings -> Users & Groups -> Login Options is another approach mentioned in the community.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about running Docker Desktop on Mac with multiple user accounts:

## Key Limitation

Docker Desktop can only be run by one user-account per machine, namely the one specified in the --user flag. This is an important architectural limitation of Docker Desktop for Mac.

## The Problem with Multiple Users

When running docker commands from a different user account on the same Macbook, the commands cannot be run, and even running with sudo the commands cannot be run—for example, running sudo docker ps results in "Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock".

After installing Docker, or updating, the docker cli commands are only accessible to the user which initiated the install/update process.

## Workarounds

Some users have documented workarounds to enable multi-user access:

- One workaround is to remove /usr/local/bin/docker and replace it with a symlink directly to /Applications/Docker.app/Contents/Resources/bin/docker

- Creating a docker user that does not need admin access, and ensuring that this user is automatically logged into in Settings -> Users & Groups -> Login Options is another approach mentioned in the community.

However, it's important to note that Docker is not designed to be securely shared among multiple users, and anyone who has access to Docker is effectively equivalent to 'root'.

content.text:
You *can*, but it won't give you what you want. Docker Desktop on macOS is a **single-user singleton at runtime**:

- The app itself (`/Applications/Docker.app`) is already there — nothing to reinstall.
- You'd just launch it while logged in as `bhyslop` on her Mac. It would spin up a **separate VM and separate state** under `~bhyslop/Library/Containers/com.docker.docker` (your own images/containers, not Ann's).
- **But** `/var/run/docker.sock` and `/usr/local/bin/docker` are system-wide symlinks that rebind to whoever launched Docker Desktop most recently. Only one Docker Desktop VM can own the socket at a time.
- So as soon as Ann next launches Docker Desktop, the symlinks flip to her and your SSH `docker` calls break — and vice versa. You'd be in a tug-of-war over `/var/run/docker.sock`.
- Also, Docker Desktop needs a **logged-in Aqua session** to keep its UI process alive. If you log out of the console, your Docker Desktop quits. SSH-only sessions won't keep it up.

Community workarounds exist (dedicated auto-login `docker` user, chmod helpers) but they're all "additional setup" and fragile.

The `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://annsegeren@anns-mba` route avoids every one of these traps: her Docker Desktop stays the one canonical instance, no tug-of-war, no auto-login user, and works from your laptop without you ever touching her console again.

Sources:
- [Docker Desktop for Mac does not work for multiple users (docker/for-mac #6781)](https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/6781)
- [Problem when multiple users on the same host want to use docker (docker/for-mac #929)](https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/929)
- [Running Docker under multiple OSX Accounts/Logins (forum)](https://forums.docker.com/t/running-docker-under-multiple-osx-accounts-logins-solved/10462)
- [Any plan to support system-wide install on Mac, or a multi-user setup? (forum)](https://forums.docker.com/t/any-plan-to-support-system-wide-install-on-mac-or-a-multi-user-setup/16658)
- [Persistent multi-user Docker on macOS (dev.to)](https://dev.to/tstromberg/persistent-multi-user-docker-on-macos-32em)

# MCP Server Aggregation Constraint in Claude Code

**Date:** 2026-03-18
**Severity:** High — affects operational architecture and context accounting
**Domain:** Claude Code MCP integration, context display behavior
**Status:** Documented constraint (not a JJK or project-side bug)

---

## Summary

Claude Code's `/context` command displays MCP tools individually rather than aggregating them by server. Multiple tools from a single MCP server appear as separate line items in the context accounting, when by MCP design they should roll up as a single server entry.

This is a **Claude Code limitation**, not an architectural flaw in our MCP implementation. Both tunneled and non-tunneled MCP command architectures exhibit identical behavior, confirming the issue is at the server registration/display layer, not the command dispatching layer.

**Key constraint:** We cannot rely on `/context` to accurately represent MCP server resource usage. Each tool appears individually, making context budgeting unclear.

---

## Observed Behavior

### Symptom
Running `/context` shows MCP tools as a flat list with individual token counts:
```
MCP tools · /mcp
└ mcp__vvx__jjx: 285 tokens
└ mcp__vvx__jjx_test_echo: 135 tokens
```

### Expected Behavior (By MCP Design)

# Remote Docker Access to Ann's MacBook via DOCKER_HOST=ssh://

**Date:** 2026-04-19
**Domain:** APCK deployment infrastructure; Docker Desktop on macOS multi-user
**Status:** Decision recorded — plan to implement

---

## Summary

To run Docker commands on `anns-macbook-air` from my laptop without fighting Docker Desktop's per-user socket ownership, the plan is:

1. Install my SSH public key into `/Users/annsegeren/.ssh/authorized_keys` on her Mac (one-time).
2. Add an SSH alias (e.g. `anns-mba`) in `~/.ssh/config` pointing at `annsegeren@<host>`.
3. Use Docker's native SSH transport: `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://annsegeren@anns-mba docker …` (or `docker -H ssh://…`).

No Docker Desktop reconfiguration. No TCP exposure. No chmod on the socket. No sudoers rule.

---

## Why This, Not the Alternatives

### What I observed

Docker Desktop 29.4.0 installed cleanly on her Mac. After first-launch privileged-helper setup, `/var/run/docker.sock` exists as a root-owned symlink to `/Users/annsegeren/.docker/run/docker.sock`, and `/usr/local/bin/docker` is symlinked to the app's CLI.

But my SSH login is `bhyslop@anns-macbook-air`, not `annsegeren`. The underlying socket is mode `srwxr-xr-x` owned by `annsegeren:staff` — others do **not** get write. So `docker version` from `bhyslop` returns "permission denied while trying to connect to the docker API at unix:///var/run/docker.sock".

### Options I ruled out

- **`sudo chmod 666` the socket.** Works for one session, but Docker Desktop recreates the socket on every VM start with Ann's perms. Also requires her admin password each time — no passwordless sudo on her machine.
- **Expose daemon on `tcp://localhost:2375`.** This toggle is Windows-only in Docker Desktop. **It has never existed on macOS** — not a v29.4.0 removal, an architectural gap Docker has acknowledged for years (docker/for-mac #6263).
- **socat TCP-bridge sidecar container.** Works, but more moving parts and still requires Ann to keep a bridge container running.
- **NOPASSWD sudoers rule to run `docker` as `annsegeren`.** Invasive, security-sensitive, and per-command.
- **Install/launch Docker Desktop under my own account on her Mac.** Docker Desktop is a single-user singleton at runtime: `/var/run/docker.sock` and `/usr/local/bin/docker` are system-wide symlinks that rebind to whichever user launched Docker Desktop most recently. Ann and I would be in a tug-of-war over the socket every time either of us launched the app. Also: Docker Desktop requires a logged-in Aqua session to keep running — an SSH-only session for `bhyslop` wouldn't sustain it. Docker's docs state plainly that "Docker is not designed to be securely shared among multiple users" (docker/for-mac #6781, #929).

### Why SSH transport wins

- **Docker-native.** `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://…` is the officially supported remote-access mechanism. No reconfig of the daemon.
- **No socket-permission fight.** The `docker` CLI on my machine forks `ssh` to her machine and talks to her socket **as her user** — which is exactly who owns it.
- **Survives reboots and Docker Desktop restarts.** Nothing to re-apply.
- **Keeps her Docker Desktop canonical.** One VM, one owner, no singleton thrash.
- **Zero ongoing human-in-the-loop.** No password prompts after the one-time key install.

---

## Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Append my public key (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`) to `/Users/annsegeren/.ssh/authorized_keys` on her Mac. Ensure permissions: `~/.ssh` = 700, `authorized_keys` = 600, owned by `annsegeren`.
- [ ] Verify `sshd_config` allows `annsegeren` (it currently allows `bhyslop`; confirm `AllowUsers` / `AllowGroups` isn't blocking her).
- [ ] Add to my `~/.ssh/config`:
  ```
  Host anns-mba
      HostName <annsegeren's host/IP>
      User annsegeren
      IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  ```
- [ ] Smoke test: `ssh anns-mba whoami` → `annsegeren`.
- [ ] Smoke test: `DOCKER_HOST=ssh://anns-mba docker version` → both Client and Server sections populate.
- [ ] Smoke test image transfer: `docker save myimage:tag | docker -H ssh://anns-mba load`.

---

## Caveats to Remember

- SSH transport requires a reachable SSH endpoint. If her Mac sleeps or the network drops, commands fail until she's back online.
- Image build context is streamed over SSH; large build contexts will be slow. For large images, consider `docker save | ssh … docker load` instead of remote `docker build`.
- Targets must match her CPU: she is `arm64` (Apple M2). Build `linux/arm64` images or multi-arch manifests.

---

## Sources

- [Docker Desktop for MacOS has no "Expose daemon on tcp://localhost:2375" setting (docker/for-mac #6263)](https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/6263)
- [Docker Desktop for Mac does not work for multiple users (docker/for-mac #6781)](https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/6781)
- [Problem when multiple users on the same host want to use docker (docker/for-mac #929)](https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/929)
- [Configure remote access for Docker daemon (Docker Docs)](https://docs.docker.com/engine/daemon/remote-access/)
- [Mac permission requirements — Docker Desktop](https://docs.docker.com/desktop/setup/install/mac-permission-requirements/)
